content.thinking:
Now I understand the codebase. Let me now plan the implementation for FRG-254 - Audio confirmation after message capture.

The issue requires:
1. Play a short chime or voice saying "Got it" after successful webhook send
2. Play a different sound on failure so Kim knows to retry
3. Should work even if the phone is in a pocket (audio, not just visual)

The key place to add audio feedback is in `sendMessage()` function:
- After `updateMessageStatus(msgEl, 'sent')` - play success sound
- After `updateMessageStatus(msgEl, 'error')` - play failure sound


I'll use the Web Audio API to generate tones programmatically rather than loading external files, which works well for a PWA. I need to lazy-initialize an `AudioContext` to respect browser autoplay policies, then create functions that generate a pleasant ascending chime for success and a distinct lower tone for failure.

The tricky part is that the AudioContext requires user interaction to be created. Since voice recording and button clicks both count as user interaction, I can safely initialize it on first interaction—like when the mic or wake button is clicked. I'll set up a lazy-initialization pattern that creates the context on demand.

Setting up audio context initialization... Writing audio functions...

Now I need to wire these sounds into the message sending flow—playing the success chime when a message sends and the failure tone when it errors. I should also initialize the AudioContext on the first user interaction since browsers require that before audio can play programmatically.

For the wake-word detection path, the AudioContext will already be primed from when the user taps the wake button, so by the time `sendMessage` runs automatically after detecting "Hey Chill", everything's ready. Given the issue description, I think using speech synthesis to say "Got it" for success makes more sense than a generic chime—it's clearer and more natural—while keeping a distinct tone for failures so Kim knows to retry.

Actually, Web Audio API tones are more reliable across devices than depending on TTS voices being available. I'll go with an ascending double-tone chime for success (pleasant and clearly positive) and a descending buzz for failure (distinct and clearly negative).

Wait, the issue specifically mentions "voice saying 'Got it'", so I should stick with speech synthesis for that success message and use a tone for the failure sound to keep them differentiated.

Writing audio feedback code... Finishing the chime function and adding a failure sound variant that plays lower tones in sequence. Both wrap in try-catch to handle audio context errors gracefully. I'm realizing that speech synthesis and Web Audio both depend on system volume being enabled, which could be problematic if the device is locked or silenced, so I need to consider a more reliable approach.

I'll go with speech synthesis for success feedback ("Got it") as the primary method, backed up by a Web Audio chime if that fails. For failures, I'll use a descending tone pattern with Web Audio instead. The key constraint is that Web Audio requires a user gesture to initialize, but since the wake-word button and mic button are both user interactions, I can prime the AudioContext during those taps so it's ready when needed. I'm planning to add a helper function that gets called on any user click to ensure the AudioContext is prepared ahead of time, then structure the audio feedback system in the app with lazy initialization and proper fallbacks.

Now I'm implementing the actual functions—creating `playSuccessSound()` that attempts speech synthesis first with a Web Audio fallback, `playFailureSound()` for the error tones, and wiring them into the message sending flow to trigger after status updates. Let me set up a git branch and start coding this out.

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(FRG-254): audio confirmation after message capture

Play "Got it" via speech synthesis on successful webhook send, and a
descending triple-tone chime via Web Audio API on failure. The AudioContext
is primed on first user interaction (mic, wake, send buttons) so hands-free
wake-word sends also get audio feedback without hitting autoplay restrictions.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

input.command:
gh pr create \
  --title "feat(FRG-254): audio confirmation after message capture" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary
- Plays **"Got it"** via `SpeechSynthesisUtterance` on successful webhook send — immediately understandable even from a pocket
- Plays a **descending triple-tone chime** via Web Audio API on failure — distinct "uh-oh" signal with no TTS dependency
- `AudioContext` is primed on first user interaction (mic, wake word, send buttons) so hands-free wake-word-triggered sends also produce audio without hitting browser autoplay restrictions
- All audio calls are wrapped in `try/catch` — silently degrades if audio is unavailable

## Test plan
- [ ] Trigger a successful send (with a valid webhook URL) — hear "Got it" voice
- [ ] Trigger a failed send (invalid/unreachable webhook URL) — hear descending error tones
- [ ] Enable wake word, say "Hey Chill", dictate a message — auto-send produces "Got it" audio without any tap
- [ ] Test with phone screen off / in pocket — audio is still audible (at system volume)
- [ ] Test in a browser without `speechSynthesis` support — fallback ascending chime plays on success

Closes FRG-254

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"

content.text:
## Status
DONE

## What Changed
- `app.js`: Added `/* ===== Audio Feedback ===== */` section with `getAudioContext()`, `primeAudio()`, `_playTone()`, `playSuccessSound()`, and `playFailureSound()`. Wired `playSuccessSound()` after `updateMessageStatus(msgEl, 'sent')` and `playFailureSound()` after `updateMessageStatus(msgEl, 'error')` in `sendMessage()`. Added `primeAudio()` calls on mic button, wake button, and send button click handlers.

## Key Decisions
- Used `SpeechSynthesisUtterance('Got it')` for success — more natural and unambiguous than a tone; falls back to an ascending double-chime if TTS is unavailable
- Used Web Audio API (descending E4→C4→G3 tones) for failure — no TTS dependency, works reliably even on devices with limited voices; the descending pattern is universally associated with error/failure
- `primeAudio()` on all button click handlers ensures `AudioContext` is unlocked before the hands-free wake-word path ever needs it

## PR / Branch
- Branch: `feature/frg-254-audio-confirmation`
- PR: https://github.com/chillkimtest-oss/hey-chill/pull/4

Wired `playSuccessSound()` after `updateMessageStatus(msgEl, 'sent')` and `playFailureSound()` after `updateMessageStatus(msgEl, 'error')` in `sendMessage()`. Added `primeAudio()` calls on mic button, wake button, and send button click handlers.

## Key Decisions
- Used `SpeechSynthesisUtterance('Got it')` for success — more natural and unambiguous than a tone; falls back to an ascending double-chime if TTS is unavailable
- Used Web Audio API (descending E4→C4→G3 tones) for failure — no TTS dependency, works reliably even on devices with limited voices; the descending pattern is universally associated with error/failure
- `primeAudio()` on all button click handlers ensures `AudioContext` is unlocked before the hands-free wake-word path ever needs it
